image_quality_feedback: Good light, but a coin or finger for scale would help judge grain size.

candidates.name: Basalt
candidates.visual_evidence: Dark grey-black, very fine-grained groundmass with no crystals visible to the naked eye, and a couple of small rounded gas cavities near the top edge.

candidates.name: Gabbro
candidates.visual_evidence: Dark and clearly heavy-looking, but the surface may show faint interlocking mineral grains, which would point coarser than basalt.

candidates.name: Andesite
candidates.visual_evidence: Medium-to-dark grey tone with a slightly rougher matte texture; possible if there are pale specks of feldspar scattered in the groundmass.

questions.text: With a hand lens or close look, can you see individual mineral crystals, or is the rock too fine to make any out?
questions.how_to: Hold the rock in good light and look closely (a phone macro or hand lens helps) at a clean, unweathered surface.
questions.discriminates: Visible interlocking crystals point to Gabbro (coarse-grained); a crystal-free groundmass points to Basalt or Andesite (fine-grained).

questions.text: Does a drop of vinegar fizz on the rock?
questions.how_to: Put one drop of household vinegar on a clean spot and watch for tiny bubbles for a few seconds.
questions.discriminates: None of these three candidates should fizz; fizzing would rule them all out and suggest a carbonate like limestone instead.

questions.text: Does a magnet stick to it, or does a compass needle deflect when held near it?
questions.how_to: Hold a fridge magnet against several spots, or pass a compass slowly over the surface and watch the needle.
questions.discriminates: A noticeable magnetic response favors Basalt, which is often rich in magnetite, over Andesite.
